Response: MIT Computer Science and Artificial Intelligence Laboratory (CSAIL), Robot Locomotion Group (originated); primary development now by Toyota Research Institute (TRI); additional contributions from MIT, CMU, and the broader robotics community

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: Open-source C++ and Python toolbox for model-based design and verification of robotics systems; focuses on multibody dynamics, trajectory optimization, model predictive control, perception, manipulation planning, and formal verification for robustly safe robots

Response: C++, Python, other (originally MATLAB; Bazel build system)

Metric: Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Drake (named after Sir Francis Drake) started as a MATLAB toolbox at MIT for dynamic systems analysis. It was rewritten in C++ with Python bindings and is now the primary simulation and planning framework used at Toyota Research Institute. Releases monthly on the last Thursday of each month. Unusually rigorous software engineering practices for a research codebase.

Response: Exceptionally smooth pip-based installation. Monthly releases published to PyPI make it easy to keep up to date. The apt package option also available for system integration. Documentation clearly specifies supported Python and OS versions. One of the best installation experiences among the tools reviewed.

Response: automated testing, Sphinx (documentation), Doxygen (C++ API), model checking, assertions used in the code, other (lcov for code coverage; ci/cd with Bazel test suite; over 95% code coverage target)

Response: Highest correctness standards of all reviewed simulators. Tutorial Jupyter notebooks ran flawlessly. The project targets >95% code coverage. Formal verification tools (mathematical proofs via Lyapunov analysis) are a core Drake feature. Russ Tedrake's online textbook uses Drake tutorials as course material.

Response: Installation and all tutorial notebooks completed without any issues. Monthly release cycle with rigorous CI testing means each release is highly stable. No reliability issues encountered during evaluation.

Response: Excellent error handling with informative exception messages. Optimization infeasibility is caught and reported with solver status details. Type annotations throughout the Python API assist users in providing correct input types.

Response: Outstanding documentation and tutorial ecosystem. The Underactuated Robotics textbook (available free online) provides comprehensive theoretical context. Jupyter Notebook tutorials are interactive and educational. Active Slack community provides fast responses. Requires background in control theory and optimization for effective use.

Response: Best-in-class software engineering practices for a research codebase. Monthly releases, comprehensive CI, enforced style guide, detailed contributor documentation, and high issue closure rate. Toyota Research Institute investment ensures long-term maintenance.

Metric: Is API documented? (yes, no, n/a)
Overall impression? (1 .. 10)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Highly modular architecture with clear separation between multibody dynamics (MultibodyPlant), systems (LeafSystem/DiagramBuilder), optimization (MathematicalProgram), and perception. Python bindings (pydrake) mirror the C++ API. Integrates with MuJoCo for contact simulation. Supports URDF and SDFormat robot models.
